architecture.summary: Reflexion is a framework for reinforcing language agents through iterative verbal feedback rather than weight updates. The system comprises three LLM-based components: an Actor that generates actions/text conditioned on state and episodic memory, an Evaluator that scores outputs (via exact match, heuristics, or LLM classification), and a Self-Reflection model that converts sparse reward signals (binary or scalar) into natural language summaries stored in episodic memory. The Actor conditions future decisions on both short-term memory (current trajectory) and long-term memory (accumulated self-reflections, bounded to 1-3 experiences). The loop iterates until task completion or max trials.

biblio.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

claims.statement: Reflexion achieves a 91% pass@1 accuracy on the HumanEval coding benchmark, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art GPT-4 that achieves 80%
Reflexion agents improve on decision-making AlfWorld tasks over strong baseline approaches by an absolute 22% in 12 iterative learning steps
Reflexion improves on reasoning questions in HotPotQA by 20%
ReAct + Reflexion completes 130 out of 134 AlfWorld tasks using the simple heuristic to detect hallucinations and inefficient planning, while ReAct-only converges at a 22% hallucination rate
On HumanEval (Rust), Reflexion achieves 68.0% pass@1 accuracy compared to GPT-4's 60.0%
On Chain-of-Thought with ground truth context (CoT GT), Reflexion improves accuracy by 14% absolute on HotPotQA, correcting mistakes on 14% of the 39% of initially unsolved questions

Reflexion is lightweight and does not require fine-tuning the LLM, allows for more nuanced forms of feedback compared to scalar rewards, maintains explicit and interpretable episodic memory over prior experiences, and provides more explicit hints for actions in future episodes

Reflexion may still succumb to non-optimal local minima solutions; code generation is bound to the model's ability to write diverse, comprehensive tests, risking false positive and false negative evaluations; practical limitations exist for test-driven development in specifying accurate input-output mappings for non-deterministic, impure, or concurrent functions
